description: Improvements to Detailed Definitions 4.13 Detailed definitions for some 50 codes have been enhanced to provide greater clarity in the descriptions of the main tasks/duties of the occupations in order to facilitate easier coding and appropriate use of the classification. Stylistic Edits to Classification Structure and Detailed Definitions 4.14 To improve stylistic coherence across the titles in classification structure and detailed definitions, stylistic edits have been implemented. 4.15 Where multiple occupations are mentioned in the five-digit occupational title, a “/” would be used for consistency and better distinction of the different occupations. Instances of “including” and “e.g.” in the main occupational titles will be removed, with these additional titles shifted to the illustrative examples in the detailed definitions for the occupation and/or added to the alphabetical index. Titles for residual codes from the sub-major group (two-digit) to occupation (five-digit) have also been aligned in the usage of terms “Other”, “Not elsewhere classified” or “n.e.c.”. The detailed definitions for the residual codes at the unit group (four-digit) and occupation (five-digit) have also been standardised. Singapore Department of Statistics Singapore Standard Occupational Classification 2024  15 5 Linking SSOC with SFw2 5.1 To facilitate analysis and comparison between SFw related information and index. New SSOC codes have been created if necessary, and changes to the scope and titles of existing SSOC codes have been made to improve the linkage between SSOC and SFw. The alphabetical index is updated half-yearly to take into account the refresh of the SFw. 5.2 Nonetheless, it should be noted that SSOC and SFw are not directly comparable due to differences in coverage, construct and purpose. SSOC is a statistical classification that is adapted from ISCO to ensure international comparability and covers all occupations whereas SFw provides information on sectors, new and changing job roles, as well as in- demand and emerging skills required for the job roles. 6 How to Determine the Appropriate Occupational Code for an Occupation 6.1 There are two ways to find an appropriate SSOC code in SSOC 2024: (a) Refer to the alphabetical index or use the SingStat SSOC Search to look for the job title by which the occupation is known, and select the most appropriate SSOC code by referring to the detailed definition. Consideration should be given to possible synonyms and abbreviations of the words in the job title; or (b) Refer to the classification structure to classify the job broadly, either at the sub-major, minor group or unit group level before narrowing down to the most appropriate five-digit code at the occupational level. The choice of SSOC code should be confirmed by referring to the detailed definition. 2 The Skills Framework (SFw) is an integral component of the Singapore’s Industry Transformation Maps. It aims to create a common skills language for individuals, employers training providers and tripartite partners which facilitates skills recognition and support the design of training programmes for skills and career development, enhance business competitiveness and support employment and employability. As at end-December 2023, there were 35 sectoral SFws and over 1,790 job roles covering more than two- thirds of the workforce. 16  Singapore Department of Statistics Singapore Standard Occupational Classification 2024 Part II: Classification Structure of SSOC Singapore Department of Statistics Singapore Standard Occupational Classification 2024  17 Major Group 1: Legislators, Senior Officials and Managers Major Group 1: Legislators, Senior Officials and Managers 1 LEGISLATORS, SENIOR OFFICIALS AND MANAGERS 11 LEGISLATORS, SENIOR OFFICIALS AND CHIEF EXECUTIVES 111 LEGISLATORS AND SENIOR OFFICIALS 1111 Legislators

description: Singapore Department of Statistics Singapore Standard Occupational Classification 2024  13 Creation of New Codes for Other New Occupations 4.7 Further to the above, to cater to the increase in occupations in certain fields with main tasks and duties that are distinct from existing occupations in the SSOC as well as new occupations becoming more prevalent in emerging industries, separate five-digit codes have been created for them in the SSOC 2024. This includes new five-digit codes created for agriculturist and aquaculturist, which were previously subsumed in the residual code. A new code has also been created for non-financial risk analyst as such role becomes increasingly distinct from the generic policy planning professionals. A separate code has also been created for executive secretary in acknowledgement of the higher skill level required compared to the usual secretaries to perform the additional specialised organisational and administrative tasks to support top executives. 4.8 To better reflect the different tasks and duties performed by credit and loans officers from that of other financial services back-office associate professional support roles, a new four-digit unit group has been created to distinguish these roles which typically perform back-office transactions and operational support, from credit and loans officers who analyse and evaluate financial information on applications for credit. Creation of New Three-digit Minor Group for Social Services, Counselling and Related Professionals 4.9 A new three-digit Minor Group 266 ‘Social Services, Counselling and Related Professionals’ has been created to separately group social services related professionals, which were previously classified in the same minor group as social science researchers and religious professionals. This is in recognition of the growing importance of Care Economy in Singapore and also enables a larger variety of occupations to be identified in the social services sector. For instance, a new four-digit Unit Group 2664 ‘Employment Support Professionals’ has been created under this minor group to accommodate emerging occupations such as job coach (disability) who provides assistance to persons with disabilities to secure and retain employment. Reclassification of Occupations Across Major Groups and Unit Groups 4.10 Some occupations have been reclassified across unit groups, taking into consideration the main tasks and duties of these occupations in the context of Singapore and developments in recent years. For instance, ‘multimedia games designer’ and ‘interaction designer’ have been reclassified from Unit Group 2166 to Unit Group 2512 given that the nature of work is more related to information and communications technology (ICT). Another example is the reclassification of ‘Educarer’ from Major Group 5 to Major Group 3. This takes into account that other than just providing care and supervision, educarers also plan and conduct educational and play activities that are intended to promote the development of children below primary school-going age, in line with the tasks of preschool educators in Major Group 3. 14  Singapore Department of Statistics Singapore Standard Occupational Classification 2024 Removal of Obsolete Codes 4.11 SSOC codes that are obsolete or have low employment counts have been removed. This is to ensure that the SSOC remains relevant and allows for new codes to be created to meet users’ needs. About 20 obsolete five-digit codes, mostly in Major Groups 7 and 8, have been merged into approximately 16 five-digit codes. Improvements to Alphabetical Index 4.12 Some 1,000 new alphabetical index entries have been added to facilitate easier coding. For comprehensiveness and ease of identifying occupational titles without the need to cross check between multiple documents, SSOC occupational titles have been added to the alphabetical index. They are labelled “Principal Title” to distinguish them from the alternative titles in the alphabetical index. An additional of some 270 new job titles have been added in the index by including constituent job titles from main occupational titles to facilitate the use of the index for coding. For example, SSOC 13302 ‘Software and applications manager’ will have “Software development manager” and “Application development manager” included in the alphabetical index as shown in the below table.

DEPARTMENT OF STATISTICS COMMUNICATIONS & INFORMATION SERVICES Singapore Department of Statistics (DOS) offers official statistics, data insights, visualisations and statistical resources through user-friendly platforms. SingStat Website Visit this convenient gateway to access latest data and statistical information. Data across the six major themes of Economy , Industry, Trade & Investment, Population, Households and Society, covering & Prices some 200topics such as Gross Domestic Product, Consumer Price Index, Retail Sales and Food & Beverage Services Indices, Producer Price Indices, Business Expectations, Business Receipts Index, Trade in Services, Direct Investment, Population Indicators, Household Income, Household Expenditure, Deaths and Life Expectancy, Births and Fertility, Marriages and Divorces, and more. Publications Information Papers which highlight conceptual and methodological changes and improvements in official statistics. Occasional Papers which present statistical trends and observations on important social and economic issues to stimulate discussion and research. eBook of Statistics which consolidates latest information and resources on key economic and socio-demographic domains. Singapore in Figures which allows exploration of key economic and socio-demographic statistics through interactive dashboards. Statistics Singapore Newsletter which features articles on latest key statistical activities, recent survey findings and methodological improvements. Visualisations such as infographics and interactive dashboards with latest data, and videos to explain data and statistical concepts. Data for Businesses with data tools to empower data-driven business decisions, giving firms insights on their customers, industries and business performance. Advance Release Calendar providing dates of upcoming data releases in the next six months. SingStat Table Builder Access for free over 2,200 data tables across various economic and socio-demographic domains from 70 public sector agencies. Watch the video to find out how you can easily access and customise data tables. SingStat Mobile App (cid:31)(cid:30)(cid:29)(cid:28)(cid:27)(cid:30)(cid:26)(cid:25) Download the app for fast and convenient access to commonly referenced statistics your mobile devices. Over 260 charts are available across via 38 data categories for easy data trends. visualisation of SingStat Express Subscribe to receive alerts on the latest press releases, occasional/information papers and newsletters. Find us on social media! For statistical enquiries, you may reach us at: @SingStat email: info@singstat.gov.sg or submit the Data Request Form phone: 1800-323 8118* (Local users) or (65) 6332 7738 (Overseas users) @singstat_dos @SingStatvideo * Calls from mobile telephone lines to 1800 local toll-free number may be subject to mobile airtime charges as imposed by the relevant mobile service provider. @sg-department-of-statistics
